content:
Hi everybody,

I've been working on this patch, which specifically deals with IRQ registration and IRQ handlers.

I've tested this on real hardware, so I know it's working. IOW, I can trigger an IRQ for the device I'm testing this with and the handler will be called correctly. The problem is that it's hard to access the shared data between the process context and the IRQ context for anything non-trivial.

My initial idea was to share an Arc<SpinLockIrq<Data>>, but @Alice Ryhl pointed out that this would create a circular reference, since we will usually have to go back to the driver's main structure for a variety of reasons:

/// Data shared between process context and IRQ context.
struct Data {
     // We need to be able to get back to `MyDriver`.
     // For example, we might need to enqueue work on a workqueue
     // or read and write to some iomem that is owned by `struct MyDriver`.
     driver: Arc<MyDriver>
}

struct MyDriver {
    // The data we are sharing between the IRQ and the rest of the driver.
    data: Arc<SpinLockIrq<Data>>

    // `MyDriver` holds a reference to the registration so that it's not
    // dropped while the driver is alive.
    // The registration holds a reference to `Data`, and therefore to`MyDriver` so
    // the handler can access the driver's structure
    registration: Arc<irq::Registration<Handler>>
}

Alice's solution was to replicate the HasWork trait from workqueue.rs to give access to MyDriver without the Arc in Data, but it's a bit unclear what to do after getting a pointer to MyDriver. Sure this cannot simply be cast to a mutable reference.

Any tips here? This is actually important if we want to be able to handle interrupts, which will be absolutely required for the majority of hardware we plan to support.

One complication compared to the workqueue is that the irq handler can trigger many times, whereas a workqueue item is only triggered once. This makes the cleanup logic much less pleasant.

@Andreas Hindborg Does your timer series support timers that trigger multiple times? What did you do there?
What is the difficulty @Daniel Almeida ?
Maybe I missed something in hrtimer

Alice Ryhl said:

@Andreas Hindborg Does your timer series support timers that trigger multiple times? What did you do there?

In hrtimer we return a handle when we start a timer. The existence of the handle ensures the data accessed by the timer callback is live. When the handle is dropped, the timer is cancelled.

One of the examples I am using below. Once SpinLockIrq is upstream and working, I will move it over to that.

use kernel::{
    time::hrtimer::{ClockSource, Timer, TimerCallback, TimerMode, TimerPointer, TimerRestart},
    impl_has_timer, new_condvar, new_mutex,
    prelude::*,
    sync::{Arc, ArcBorrow, CondVar, Mutex},
    time::Ktime,
};

#[pin_data]
struct ArcIntrusiveTimer {
    #[pin]
    timer: Timer<Self>,
    #[pin]
    flag: Mutex<u64>,
    #[pin]
    cond: CondVar,
}

impl ArcIntrusiveTimer {
    fn new() -> impl PinInit<Self, kernel::error::Error> {
        try_pin_init!(Self {
            timer <- Timer::new(TimerMode::Relative, ClockSource::Monotonic),
            flag <- new_mutex!(0),
            cond <- new_condvar!(),
        })
    }
}

impl TimerCallback for ArcIntrusiveTimer {
    type CallbackTarget<'a> = Arc<Self>;
    type CallbackTargetParameter<'a> = ArcBorrow<'a, Self>;

    fn run(this: Self::CallbackTargetParameter<'_>) -> TimerRestart {
        pr_info!("Timer called\n");
        let mut guard = this.flag.lock();
        *guard += 1;
        this.cond.notify_all();
        if *guard == 5 {
            TimerRestart::NoRestart
        }
        else {
            TimerRestart::Restart

        }
    }
}

impl_has_timer! {
    impl HasTimer<Self> for ArcIntrusiveTimer { self.timer }
}


let has_timer = Arc::pin_init(ArcIntrusiveTimer::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?;
let _handle = has_timer.clone().start(Ktime::from_ns(200_000_000));
let mut guard = has_timer.flag.lock();

while *guard != 5 {
    has_timer.cond.wait(&mut guard);
}

pr_info!("Counted to 5\n");
@Daniel Almeida why do you need a back reference from Data to MyDriver ? I assume some kind of Data reference is passed to the irq handler when it fires?
If you need to access MyDriver from the irq handler, why not pass a MyDriver reference to the irq handler when it fires and use that to access Data. Then you get rid of the cycle.

For rnvme we are doing something similar. I am not sure how different the irq registration logic in the rnvme tree is compared to yours. But we just implement the handler on the top level data structure we want to access and then we get a graph without cycles: https://github.com/metaspace/linux/blob/1f702e2d9d45df0e4457b394c7c03df8cec1ff70/drivers/block/nvme_queue.rs#L253

@Andreas Hindborg yeah I added a few comments explaining why we need to go back, basically:

/// Data shared between process context and IRQ context.
struct Data {
     // We need to be able to get back to `MyDriver`.
     // For example, we might need to enqueue work on a workqueue
     // or read and write to some iomem that is owned by `struct MyDriver`.
     driver: Arc<MyDriver>
}

If you need to access MyDriver from the irq handler, why not pass a MyDriver reference to the irq handler when it fires and use that to access Data. Then you get rid of the cycle.

That is the same cycle, the IRQ handler would have a reference to MyDriver, and MyDriver would have a reference to the IRQ through irq::Registration.

With a HasWork-like implementation you can get a *mut MyDriver when the handle fires. The question is what to do what that, can this simply be cast to &mut MyDriver?
Right. In rnull I get around that by leaking the timer handle.
Which is similar to your registration.
I can do that because I know the timer will fire, and since the handle owns the data referenced in the timer callback, it will not go away.
Not really an option for you though.
It's the same for the IRQ stuff, we can be sure that MyDriver is not going to go away, since it will call free_irq() on drop, which will block until all handlers complete
You can't get rid of the registration, right?
That would unregister the irq.
yeah, that would unregister (free_irq)
One option would be to implement weak references for Arc, or use a revocable resource type for one of the directions.
I can't follow the HasWork approach. How would that work?
You would have a HasXX trait that guarantees that MyDriver has a Data embedded somewhere?
And they you would store a pointer to MyDriver in Data?
And you know that MyDriver is live, because the irq handler is running.
we can use HasIrqHandler to get an offset in the containing structure, and use something akin to container_of to go back to *mut MyDriver without any Arcs involved
Right.
MyDriver OTOH, would be able to access the data directly
You can only turn that pointer into a mutable reference if you can guarantee that it is unique though.
You still need a lock of some kind, but I guess that is OK

you can register multiple handlers :/

if both fire at the same time (which is possible if they're using two distinct IRQ lines, electrically) then you get more than one &mut MyDriver
(I just realized this)
That would be UB. But you can hand out immutable references instead and then synchronize to provide interior mutability.

For hrtimer we allow mutable access when the target is a Box, because it is a unique pointer, and while the timer is running, the box is owned by the timer handle. The timer handle stores the box as a raw pointer and promises not to crate any references to the contents while the timer is running.
The only lock possible is SpinLockIrq, since you can't sleep in interrupt context. We can't wrap MyDriver in SpinLockIrq
You only need to wrap the parts that you need concurrent access to.
Basically, you would have a trait bound on HasIrq: Sync
Or something like that.
Btw, what about the weak references you suggested ?
I wonder how others feel about this
That is how shared pointers usually solve cycles. Std library Arc has them.
Our Arc has this comment: "//! 2. It does not support weak references, which allows it to be half the size."
Perhaps we could have another type that supports weak references.
But the price you pay as a user is that accesses may fail for weak refcounts. So you basically have to check if the target is still present before accessing.
I can look into that, if it's generally agreed that it's worthwhile
I think that would solve the problem here
with the least amount of "magic"

maybe we can discuss this at the next call?

(cc @Boqun Feng )

Daniel Almeida said:

you can register multiple handlers :/

if both fire at the same time (which is possible if they're using two distinct IRQ lines, electrically) then you get more than one &mut MyDriver

That is exactly why converting a pointer to a mutable reference is unsafe. In this case, you cannot use a &mut MyDriver, because two contexts can access the same object.
Ok, so maybe the Weak thing?

struct MyDriver {
    // The data we are sharing between the IRQ and the rest of the driver.
    data: Arc<SpinLockIrq<Data>>

    // `MyDriver` holds a reference to the registration so that it's not
    // dropped while the driver is alive.
    // The registration holds a reference to `Data`, and therefore to`MyDriver` so
    // the handler can access the driver's structure
    registration: Arc<irq::Registration<Handler>>
}

Why Arc<irq::Registration<Handler>> here, who else is holding the reference to the registration?

Daniel Almeida said:

Ok, so maybe the Weak thing?

Even with Weak reference count, you cannot get a &mut MyDriver in this case.
Why do you want a &mut MyDriver then? In this case, it's clear that MyDriver is shared between interrupt handlers, so you will have to implement proper interior mutability as what Andreas suggested.

Why Arc<irq::Registration<Handler>> here, who else is holding the reference to the registration?

The idea was to offer more flexibility, so other parts of the driver could also access the registration and thus, the shared data, i.e.:

///
/// impl kernel::irq::Handler for Handler {
///     // This is executing in IRQ context in some CPU. Other CPUs can still
///     // try to access to data.
///     fn handle_irq(&self) -> irq::IrqReturn {
///         // We now have exclusive access to the data by locking the SpinLock.
///         let mut handler = self.lock();
///         handler.0 += 1;
///
///         IrqReturn::Handled
///     }
/// }
///
/// // This is running in process context.
/// fn register_irq(irq: u32, handler: Handler) -> Result<irq::Registration<Handler>> {
///     let registration = Registration::register(irq, irq::flags::SHARED, "my-device", handler)?;
///
///     // You can have as many references to the registration as you want, so
///     // multiple parts of the driver can access it.
///     let registration = Arc::pin_init(registration)?;
///
///     // The handler may be called immediately after the function above
///     // returns, possibly in a different CPU.
///
///     // The data can be accessed from the process context too.
///     registration.handler().lock().0 = 42;
///
///     Ok(registration)
/// }

Sorry, I guess I lost track a bit here,

a) I'm trying to solve the circular reference
b) Alice suggested the HasWork trait as an possible solution,
c) Using something like HasIrqHandler lets you get *mut MyDriver
d) I didn't know what to do with this pointer, i.e.: whether I should cast that and etc
e) Among other things, Weak was suggested.

I forgot that Weak would return a shared reference, which is why there's some confusion now with &mut MyDriver. But then, even with a shared reference, we can still use SpinLockIrq to allow mutating the data.

Well, even without reference count like:

struct MyDriver {
    data: ...
    registration: irq::Registration<Handler>,
}

multiple parts of the driver can access via:

let my_driver: &MyDriver = ...;
do_sth(my_driver.registration);
True, the above is trivial, the problem is doing the same from the handler.
You mean that I should remove the Arc from Registration ?
Weak would get rid of the reference count cycle. Still need synchronization.

Daniel Almeida said:

You mean that I should remove the Arc from Registration ?

We need to look into a usage, there are a few design options here.
HasIrqHandler similarly would get rid of the cycle, but we would still need synchronization

but honestly, I think you could define Data as the whole driver data to begin with. That is, all the data fields of a driver is in Data, therefore you don't need to reference back to MyDriver from Data.

more like:

struct Data {
    work_head: Work<Data>,
    f1: SpinLock<i32>,
    ...
}

struct MyDriver {
    data: Arc<Data>,

    registration: ...
}

@Boqun Feng here's an example usage:

You can see that the first thing done is to get struct panthor_device *.

This is piped down to a more polished version after some housekeeping, like this handler here:

Access to panthor_device * is needed to do everything, basically, including IO reads and writes, waking up tasks and etc.

Synchronization is done using spinlocks (i.e. spin_lock(), spin_lock_irqsave(), etc)

and

struct MyDriverHandler1(Arc<Data>);

impl kernel::irq::Handler for MyDriverHandler1 {
    fn handle_irq(&self) -> irq::ThreadedIrqReturn {
        let data: &Arc<Data> = self.0;
        do_sth(data);
    }
}

Daniel Almeida said:

Boqun Feng here's an example usage:

You can see that the first thing done is to get struct panthor_device *.

This is piped down to a more polished version after some housekeeping, like this handler here:

Access to panthor_device * is needed to do everything, basically, including IO reads and writes, waking up tasks and etc.

Synchronization is done using spinlocks (i.e. spin_lock(), spin_lock_irqsave(), etc)

So panthor_device is your Data, and you just need to make sure it doesn't reference back a driver, right?

but honestly, I think you could define Data as the whole driver data to begin with. That is, all the data fields of a driver is in Data, therefore you don't need to reference back to MyDriver from Data.

Ok, I think this makes sense and reduces the complexity.

So panthor_device is your Data, and you just need to make sure it doesn't reference back a driver, right?

If we do what you said above, then yes, panthor_device would be Data, or at least I do not see why this can't be the case now.

we cannot wrap Data into SpinLockIrq though, it has to be more granular than that, because we may (and often will) access the contents without locking (if we know it's safe to do so, given the state of the hardware at that point)
Yes, we shouldn't in this case.
or wouldn't ?
I think in general we shouldn't impose any locks, it's up to users to decide if they want interior mutability, and where they want that
Ok, so I think that your suggestion basically solves the problem even without Weak
Hey - poking this again, what is currently the status of this? Since we've got two drivers interested in having this now
@Lyude there' s a recent-ish version at https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250122163932.46697-1-daniel.almeida@collabora.com/
ah whoops - I meant to ask this on the spinlockirq thread
